news_summary: Astronomers have made groundbreaking discoveries linking atmospheric conditions on habitable rocky planets, early prediction of solar storms, quantum gravity's connection to dark energy and life, and new insights into the motion of dying Sun-like stars.
news_summary_fr: Les astronomes ont fait des découvertes révolutionnaires établissant un lien entre les conditions atmosphériques sur les planètes rocheuses habitables, la prévision précoce des tempêtes solaires, le rapport entre la gravité quantique, l'énergie noire et la vie, ainsi que de nouvelles perspectives sur le mouvement des étoiles de type solaire en fin de vie.
news_summary_es: Los astrónomos han realizado descubrimientos revolucionarios que relacionan las condiciones atmosféricas de los planetas rocosos habitables, la predicción temprana de las tormentas solares, la conexión entre la gravedad cuántica y la energía oscura y la vida, y nuevos conocimientos sobre el movimiento de las estrellas similares al Sol que se encuentran en fase terminal.

articles.title: Astronomers find the first atmosphere on a rocky world in the habitable zone
articles.summary: Astronomers have found the first confirmed atmosphere around a rocky planet in another star’s habitable zone. The planet, LHS 1140 b, revealed its atmosphere through helium slowly leaking into space. Located 48 light-years away, the world may have preserved its atmosphere for billions of years, making it a promising target in the search for potentially habitable planets.
articles.content:
Astronomers have reached a major turning point in the search for life beyond Earth by detecting an atmosphere around a rocky, Earth-like planet located in another star's habitable zone.

The discovery offers the strongest evidence so far that planets with broadly Earth-like temperatures and rocky compositions may exist beyond our solar system while also retaining conditions that could potentially support life.

"An atmosphere is essential for a planet to support life as we know it," said lead author Collin Cherubim, who recently earned his Ph.D. in Earth and Planetary Sciences from Harvard University.

"This is the first time anyone has found an atmosphere on a rocky planet in the habitable zone of another star."

Helium Reveals an Exoplanet Atmosphere

The study, published July 16 in Science, describes observations of helium escaping from LHS 1140 b, a rocky exoplanet located about 48 light-years from Earth. The signal supports earlier theoretical predictions that the planet possesses an atmosphere.

LHS 1140 b circles a red dwarf star within its habitable zone. This is the region around a star where temperatures and other environmental conditions may allow liquid water to remain on a planet's surface.

Scientists have identified thousands of exoplanets, including several rocky worlds in habitable zones. However, confirming whether those planets have atmospheres has been one of the most difficult challenges in exoplanet research.

"Twenty years ago we wondered whether other terrestrial-type planets even existed," said Robin Wordsworth, Gordon McKay Professor of Environmental Science and Engineering and Professor of Earth and Planetary Sciences at Harvard and one of Cherubim's dissertation advisors. "Then we learned they're common, and found some in the habitable zone. The next question was whether any of them had managed to keep an atmosphere. Now we know at least one has."

A Rocky World That Kept Its Atmosphere

Previous research has identified rocky planets orbiting within the habitable zones of their stars. This study, however, is the first to clearly show that one of those worlds has an atmosphere that may have endured for billions of years.

Cherubim and his colleagues developed a theoretical model predicting that the upper atmosphere of LHS 1140 b would contain large amounts of helium slowly escaping into space.

The researchers tested that prediction using the Warm Infrared Echelle (WINERED) Spectrograph at the Magellan Observatory in Chile. Their observations took advantage of an unusual event in which LHS 1140 b and another planet crossed in front of their star during the same night.

The second planet showed no sign of an atmosphere. LHS 1140 b, however, produced a clear signal of helium escaping from the planet, providing evidence that it still retains an atmosphere.

A Prediction Confirmed by Telescope Data

Cherubim's joint advisor David Charbonneau, head of the Harvard Department of Astronomy and astronomer in the Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian, initially doubted whether the plan would succeed. The prediction came from a mathematical model, and this type of signal had never before been observed from a rocky world.

The results changed his mind.

"Collin analyzed the planets we knew about and predicted that this one would have a helium atmosphere," Charbonneau said. "Then he organized telescope time, got the data, and the detection was statistically rock solid."

The discovery indicates that astronomers may be able to study the atmospheres of rocky exoplanets from the ground by looking for gases escaping into space.

A Promising Target in the Search for Life

Astronomers estimate that the atmosphere of LHS 1140 b has survived for more than three billion years. That longevity makes the planet an especially promising target for additional observations.

Cherubim hopes to identify the atmosphere's complete chemical composition and eventually determine whether the planet has surface oceans or other features connected with habitability. He and his colleagues also plan to use the model to search for additional rocky worlds with atmospheres.

"This has been a model validation, and hopefully it's just the first of many more observations to come," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough: the first confirmed detection of an atmosphere on a rocky exoplanet in the habitable zone, which is a major step forward in the search for potentially habitable worlds beyond our solar system. The discovery has broad implications for astronomy and the search for life, with detailed context about the methods and significance, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers detect first atmosphere on rocky planet in habitable zone
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been searching for planets outside our solar system that might support life. These planets need to be rocky like Earth and have an atmosphere to protect and support life. Finding such an atmosphere has been very difficult so far.

**What happened** – A team of astronomers discovered the first confirmed atmosphere around a rocky planet called LHS 1140 b. This planet is about 48 light-years away and orbits within the habitable zone of its star, where conditions might allow liquid water to exist. They detected helium gas slowly escaping from the planet using a special telescope in Chile, confirming the atmosphere’s presence.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows that rocky planets outside our solar system can keep their atmospheres for billions of years. An atmosphere is important for life because it helps regulate temperature and protects the surface. This discovery means scientists can now study atmospheres of distant rocky planets from Earth, bringing us closer to finding planets that might support life.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study the atmosphere of LHS 1140 b in more detail to learn what gases it contains and whether the planet has oceans or other signs of habitability. They will also use their methods to search for other rocky planets with atmospheres.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have confirmed an atmosphere on a rocky planet in the habitable zone, opening new paths in the search for life beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes détectent la première atmosphère sur une planète rocheuse en zone habitable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire qui pourraient abriter la vie. Ces planètes doivent être rocheuses comme la Terre et posséder une atmosphère pour protéger et soutenir la vie. Trouver une telle atmosphère a été très difficile jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe d’astronomes a découvert la première atmosphère confirmée autour d’une planète rocheuse appelée LHS 1140 b. Cette planète se trouve à environ 48 années-lumière et orbite dans la zone habitable de son étoile, où les conditions peuvent permettre la présence d’eau liquide. Ils ont détecté de l’hélium s’échappant lentement de la planète grâce à un télescope spécial au Chili, confirmant ainsi la présence de l’atmosphère.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que des planètes rocheuses en dehors de notre système peuvent conserver leur atmosphère pendant des milliards d’années. Une atmosphère est essentielle à la vie car elle aide à réguler la température et protège la surface. Cette découverte signifie que les scientifiques peuvent désormais étudier les atmosphères de planètes rocheuses lointaines depuis la Terre, rapprochant la recherche de planètes potentiellement habitables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’étudier plus en détail l’atmosphère de LHS 1140 b pour connaître sa composition et voir si la planète possède des océans ou d’autres signes d’habitabilité. Ils utiliseront aussi leur méthode pour chercher d’autres planètes rocheuses avec atmosphère.

**Phrase-clé** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont confirmé une atmosphère sur une planète rocheuse en zone habitable, ouvrant de nouvelles voies dans la recherche de vie au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos detectan la primera atmósfera en un planeta rocoso en zona habitable
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar que puedan albergar vida. Estos planetas deben ser rocosos como la Tierra y tener una atmósfera que proteja y sostenga la vida. Detectar una atmósfera así ha sido muy difícil hasta ahora.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de astrónomos descubrió la primera atmósfera confirmada alrededor de un planeta rocoso llamado LHS 1140 b. Este planeta está a unos 48 años luz y orbita dentro de la zona habitable de su estrella, donde las condiciones podrían permitir agua líquida. Detectaron helio escapando lentamente del planeta usando un telescopio especial en Chile, confirmando la presencia de la atmósfera.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que planetas rocosos fuera de nuestro sistema pueden mantener sus atmósferas por miles de millones de años. Una atmósfera es clave para la vida porque ayuda a regular la temperatura y protege la superficie. Esto significa que los científicos ahora pueden estudiar atmósferas de planetas rocosos lejanos desde la Tierra, acercándonos a encontrar planetas que podrían ser habitables.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar la atmósfera de LHS 1140 b con más detalle para saber qué gases tiene y si el planeta tiene océanos u otras señales de habitabilidad. También usarán su método para buscar otros planetas rocosos con atmósfera.

**Frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos han confirmado una atmósfera en un planeta rocoso en zona habitable, abriendo nuevas vías en la búsqueda de vida fuera de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing rocky exoplanet with a thin, shimmering atmosphere gently escaping as soft golden helium streams into space, orbiting a radiant red dwarf star in a calm cosmic landscape with subtle stars and a distant telescope silhouette observing from a serene mountaintop below.

articles.title: A hidden “switch-off” signal could predict solar storms seven years early
articles.summary: A newly discovered turning point in the Sun’s 11-year cycle could allow scientists to predict future solar activity years earlier than before. At this “switch-off” point, the most violent space weather abruptly ends, and the remaining number of sunspots offers clues about the next cycle’s strength. Early projections suggest Solar Cycle 26 may be moderate, but a clearer forecast is expected in about two years.
articles.content:
Scientists have developed a forecasting technique that could reveal the strength of the Sun's next activity cycle as many as seven years before it reaches its maximum.

The method focuses on the number of sunspots present at a newly identified "switch-off" stage in the solar cycle. At this point, the Sun's most severe space weather appears to end abruptly. Researchers have already used the approach to produce an early estimate for Solar Cycle 26.

Initial projections suggest that Cycle 26 could be moderate, with a sunspot number of roughly 100 to 120. That would make it comparable to, or possibly weaker than, the current Solar Cycle 25. However, researchers will not be able to make a more precise forecast for about two years, and both stronger and weaker outcomes remain theoretically possible.

The findings are being presented this week at the Royal Astronomical Society's National Astronomy Meeting in Birmingham.

The Sun's Extreme Weather Stops Suddenly

Sandra Chapman, Professor of Physics and Director of the Centre for Fusion, Space and Astrophysics at the University of Warwick, said: "The Sun doesn't gently go to sleep and then gently wake up again.

"Instead, we've discovered that the most extreme space weather switches off quite suddenly at a specific point in every solar cycle. By identifying that point, we've found a new way to predict how active the next solar cycle is likely to be."

Professor Chapman expects the Cycle 26 forecast to become much more accurate in about two years. By then, Solar Cycle 25 should reach the newly identified "switch off" point, allowing scientists to base their calculations on direct observations instead of projections.

The Sun moves through a cycle lasting approximately 11 years. During that time, its magnetic field reverses polarity, while the number of visible sunspots increases and then declines.

Sunspots are highly active magnetic regions on the Sun's surface. They can produce powerful solar flares and coronal mass ejections, which send energy and charged particles into space. This space weather can interfere with satellites, communications, navigation systems, and electrical power grids on Earth.

Astronomers have tracked sunspots for centuries, but every solar cycle behaves differently. Some cycles are longer or shorter than others, and their intensity can vary considerably. These differences have made it difficult to forecast the strength of an upcoming cycle.

A New Solar Clock Improves Forecasting

The new technique builds on Professor Chapman's earlier "sunclock," a system that places the Sun's irregular cycles onto a standardized clock. That work showed that extreme space weather does not slowly disappear as a cycle winds down. Instead, it ends at a clearly defined stage.

Professor Chapman and her colleagues found that the number of sunspots visible at this stage is closely connected to the highest sunspot count reached during the next solar cycle.

This relationship creates a new way to estimate the strength of a future cycle about six to seven years before it reaches its peak. Existing forecasting methods generally provide less advance notice because scientists must wait until the Sun reaches solar minimum, the quietest part of its cycle.

The technique also points to a particular phase when the magnetic field responsible for the next cycle is expected to become established. Researchers hope that this timing will help them better understand the solar dynamo, the process that creates and maintains the Sun's magnetic field.

Professor Chapman said: "We're about two years away from the switch-off point for the current Solar Cycle 25. At the moment, we have to estimate where that point will be, but once we reach it we can use observations alone to make a much more precise prediction for Solar Cycle 26.

"That will still give us around seven years' warning of how strong the cycle is likely to be."

Earlier Forecast Correctly Anticipated Cycle 25

The method previously indicated that Solar Cycle 25 would be more active than many earlier forecasts had predicted. That stronger activity helped produce the striking auroral displays seen in recent years.

The UK experienced several historic solar storms in 2024 as Cycle 25 approached its "solar maximum," the period of greatest activity. The most notable events occurred from May 10 to 13.

A cluster of enormous sunspots near solar maximum triggered the strongest geomagnetic storms to affect Earth in more than 20 years. The events produced bright and widespread auroras across the UK, with the northern lights visible as far south as Devon and Cornwall.

In 2022, Professor Chapman received the Royal Astronomical Society's Chapman Medal. The award recognized her pioneering research into the behavior of planetary magnetic fields and the ways those fields generate space weather.

Why Solar Storms Switch Off

The newly identified switch-off point also occurs when active sunspot regions move below about 15 degrees solar latitude.

During each solar cycle, sunspots form a "butterfly pattern." They first appear at higher latitudes and gradually move closer to the solar equator as the cycle progresses.

The Sun has differential rotation, meaning that different latitudes rotate at different speeds. Below about 15 degrees latitude, however, that difference in rotational speed becomes weaker. This creates a co-rotating region around the solar equator (the solar 'jet stream').

Professor Chapman believes that the most powerful coronal mass ejections are driven by differential rotation. As parts of the Sun rotate at different speeds, they twist the emerging magnetic field and build up energy. Once active sunspot regions move within 15 degrees of the equator, that twisting mechanism weakens and the main driver of extreme space weather switches off.

To test the idea, Professor Chapman examined the 27-day (average solar rotation) correlation in the aa index (geomagnetic activity at Earth) and compared it with recorded space weather events.

After the switch-off point, geomagnetic storms became less extreme and followed a 27-day pattern. This suggests they were probably being produced by co-rotating streams rather than coronal mass ejections.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in solar cycle forecasting that can predict solar storms up to seven years in advance. This advance has broad implications for protecting satellites, communications, navigation, and power grids worldwide, offering tangible benefits to society. The story is focused, detailed, and presents a meaningful improvement over previous forecasting methods.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find early signal to predict solar storms up to seven years ahead
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Sun goes through an 11-year cycle where its activity changes, including the number of sunspots on its surface. These sunspots can cause solar storms that affect Earth’s satellites, power grids, and communication systems. Predicting these storms early helps prepare for their effects.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered a new “switch-off” point in the Sun’s cycle where the most intense solar storms suddenly stop. By counting sunspots at this point, they can predict how strong the next solar cycle will be, up to seven years before it peaks. Using this method, they estimate the upcoming Solar Cycle 26 will be moderate in strength.

**Impact** – This finding offers a much earlier forecast than before, giving more time to prepare for solar storms that can disrupt technology on Earth. It also improves understanding of how the Sun’s magnetic field works, which controls solar activity. Earlier forecasts using this method correctly predicted stronger solar activity in the current cycle, showing it works well.

**What's next step** – In about two years, when the current Solar Cycle 25 reaches the switch-off point, scientists will use actual observations instead of estimates to make more accurate predictions for Solar Cycle 26. This will help confirm the method’s accuracy and refine future forecasts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly found “switch-off” signal in the Sun’s activity lets scientists predict solar storms up to seven years early, improving our ability to prepare for space weather.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un signal précoce pour prévoir les tempêtes solaires jusqu'à sept ans à l'avance
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Soleil suit un cycle de 11 ans durant lequel son activité change, notamment le nombre de taches solaires à sa surface. Ces taches peuvent provoquer des tempêtes solaires qui affectent les satellites, les réseaux électriques et les communications sur Terre. Prévoir ces tempêtes tôt aide à s’y préparer.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert un nouveau point de « coupure » dans le cycle solaire où les tempêtes solaires les plus fortes s’arrêtent soudainement. En comptant les taches solaires à ce moment, ils peuvent prédire la force du prochain cycle solaire jusqu’à sept ans avant son pic. Selon cette méthode, le prochain cycle solaire 26 devrait être d’intensité modérée.

**Impact** – Cette découverte permet de prévoir beaucoup plus tôt qu’avant, donnant plus de temps pour se préparer aux tempêtes solaires qui peuvent perturber la technologie sur Terre. Elle améliore aussi la compréhension du champ magnétique du Soleil, qui contrôle son activité. Des prévisions antérieures basées sur cette méthode ont correctement anticipé une activité solaire plus forte, prouvant son efficacité.

**Prochaine étape** – Dans environ deux ans, lorsque le cycle solaire actuel 25 atteindra le point de coupure, les scientifiques utiliseront des observations réelles au lieu d’estimations pour affiner les prévisions du cycle 26. Cela permettra de confirmer la précision de la méthode et d’améliorer les prévisions futures.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau signal de « coupure » dans l’activité solaire permet de prévoir les tempêtes solaires jusqu’à sept ans à l’avance, améliorant notre préparation aux conditions spatiales.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran señal temprana para predecir tormentas solares hasta siete años antes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Sol pasa por un ciclo de 11 años en el que cambia su actividad, incluyendo la cantidad de manchas solares en su superficie. Estas manchas pueden causar tormentas solares que afectan satélites, redes eléctricas y comunicaciones en la Tierra. Predecir estas tormentas con anticipación ayuda a prepararse.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron un nuevo punto de “apagado” en el ciclo solar donde las tormentas solares más intensas se detienen de repente. Contando las manchas solares en ese momento, pueden predecir la fuerza del siguiente ciclo solar hasta siete años antes de su máximo. Según este método, se estima que el próximo Ciclo Solar 26 será de intensidad moderada.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo permite hacer pronósticos mucho más temprano que antes, dando más tiempo para prepararse ante tormentas solares que pueden interrumpir tecnología en la Tierra. También mejora la comprensión del campo magnético del Sol, que controla su actividad. Pronósticos anteriores con este método acertaron en anticipar una actividad solar más fuerte, mostrando su eficacia.

**Próximo paso** – En unos dos años, cuando el Ciclo Solar 25 actual alcance el punto de apagado, los científicos usarán observaciones reales en lugar de estimaciones para hacer predicciones más precisas del Ciclo Solar 26. Esto ayudará a confirmar la precisión del método y mejorar futuros pronósticos.

**Frase resumen** – Una nueva señal de “apagado” en la actividad solar permite predecir tormentas solares hasta siete años antes, mejorando nuestra capacidad para prepararnos al clima espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a radiant sun with distinct, glowing sunspots clustered near its equator, surrounded by swirling magnetic field lines gently twisting and then abruptly calming at a glowing "switch-off" boundary near 15 degrees latitude, set against a soft cosmic background of muted oranges and yellows.

articles.title: New quantum gravity theory links entropy, dark energy, and life
articles.summary: A new theoretical study offers a possible explanation for how the Universe can grow more complex without violating the second law of thermodynamics. Using a quantum gravity framework called Gravity from Entropy, mathematician Ginestra Bianconi found that the Universe’s total entropy may rise as space expands, even while entropy within each unit of volume falls. That decline could leave room for galaxies, stars, planets, and life to form locally amid an overall increase in cosmic entropy.
articles.content:
A new theoretical study offers a possible way to address one of modern physics' most difficult puzzles: how did the Universe produce galaxies, stars, planets, and life while continuing to follow the second law of thermodynamics?

The research was led by Professor Ginestra Bianconi, a mathematician at Queen Mary University of London. Her work examines whether a theory known as Gravity from Entropy can help explain how cosmic complexity emerges even as the Universe's total entropy increases.

The Universe's Entropy Puzzle

Einstein famously stated that "The second law of thermodynamics occupies a unique position among the laws of Nature," reflecting his view that it was one of the most secure and fundamental principles in physics.

The second law says that the total entropy of an isolated system generally increases over time. Entropy is often described as a measure of disorder, although it more precisely reflects how energy and information can be distributed within a system.

That principle creates a major challenge for cosmology. Scientists generally think the early Universe began in a state of low entropy and has steadily moved toward higher entropy. At the same time, matter has organized itself into increasingly elaborate structures, from galaxies and stars to planets and living organisms.

Explaining how this growing complexity can coexist with the continual rise of entropy remains an unresolved problem.

A New View Through Gravity From Entropy

In a paper published in Physical Review D, Bianconi investigates the puzzle using Gravity from Entropy (GfE), a proposed approach to quantum gravity.

The theory uses ideas from statistical mechanics to describe gravity as something that emerges from the microscopic properties of spacetime geometry. Rather than treating gravity only as a fundamental force or as the curvature of spacetime, GfE connects it to information and entropy at the quantum level.

Bianconi's analysis reveals a potentially important distinction. Although the Universe's total entropy increases over time, the amount of entropy per unit of volume decreases as the Universe expands.

This behavior could offer a new way to understand how organized structures can develop locally without violating the second law of thermodynamics.

Black Holes Connected Gravity and Heat

The idea that gravity and thermodynamics are deeply linked dates back to the pioneering work of Jacob Bekenstein and Stephen Hawking in the 1970s.

Their discoveries showed that black holes have entropy and can emit thermal radiation. These findings transformed the scientific understanding of black holes and pointed to a much deeper connection among spacetime, information, gravity, and heat.

Gravity from Entropy builds on this broader relationship.

According to GfE, gravity emerges from an informational tension between the actual spacetime metric and another metric produced by matter fields and spacetime curvature. A metric is the mathematical structure used to describe distances and geometry in spacetime.

This interpretation is expressed through the GfE Lagrangian, which is defined by the Quantum Geometric Relative Entropy (QGRE) between the two metrics.

A Possible Link to Dark Energy

At low energies and under conditions of weak spacetime curvature, the equations of Gravity from Entropy reproduce General Relativity. Under more extreme conditions, however, the predictions begin to differ.

Beyond this weak limit, the GfE equations produce a changing dark energy contribution. Because this term evolves dynamically, it could potentially generate predictions that researchers might eventually test through cosmological observations.

The study examines these thermodynamic effects in Friedmann-Robertson-Walker cosmological spacetimes, which are mathematical models commonly used to describe a Universe that expands uniformly on large scales.

The results indicate that the local geometric components of spacetime obey a version of the first law of thermodynamics. In this description, the emerging dark energy contribution acts as internal energy, while the Quantum Geometric Relative Entropy (QGRE) represents the local entropy per unit of volume.

Quantities corresponding to effective temperature and pressure also arise naturally from the theory. Together, these results suggest that the quantum state underlying Gravity from Entropy may have an inherent thermal character.

Expansion Spreads Entropy Across Space

The study also emphasizes the importance of the local volume element determined by the physical spacetime metric.

As the Universe expands, its volume grows. Within the GfE framework, that increasing volume causes total entropy to rise, even as the local QGRE within each unit of volume gradually declines.

In other words, the Universe can contain more entropy overall while entropy becomes more widely distributed across expanding space. This unusual thermodynamic pattern may help clarify how localized regions of structure and complexity can arise.

Gravity and Spacetime May Be Thermodynamic

The findings support the possibility that gravity and spacetime have both informational and thermodynamic foundations.

Such an interpretation could provide new ways to investigate the relationships among gravity, quantum theory, dark energy, cosmic evolution, and the emergence of complex structures.

The proposal remains at an early theoretical stage. However, the researchers say it may contribute to efforts to connect general relativity, thermodynamics, quantum mechanics, and cosmology within a broader framework.

"This work reveals how the Gravity from Entropy theory can tackle the challenging question to reconcile the second principle of thermodynamics with the emergence of complexity in our Universe. These results may open new avenues for investigating the long-standing problem of reconciling the foundations of cosmological irreversibility, the emergence of complex structures, and ultimately life, with fundamental gravitational dynamics," says Professor Bianconi.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant theoretical breakthrough in understanding the Universe's complexity and entropy, linking quantum gravity, dark energy, and the emergence of life. This insight addresses a fundamental cosmological puzzle with broad implications for physics and our understanding of the cosmos, offering a hopeful and intellectually uplifting perspective on cosmic evolution.
articles.personality_title: New theory explains how the Universe grows complex while following physics laws
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how the Universe can become more complex, forming stars, planets, and life, while still following the second law of thermodynamics. This law says that total disorder, or entropy, usually increases over time, which seems to contradict the rise of order and complexity.

**What happened** – Mathematician Professor Ginestra Bianconi from Queen Mary University of London studied this puzzle using a theory called Gravity from Entropy (GfE). This theory connects gravity, space, and information at the smallest scales. Her research showed that although the Universe’s total entropy rises as space expands, the entropy within each small volume actually decreases. This means that local areas can become more organized even while overall disorder grows.

**Impact** – This finding offers a new way to understand how complex structures like galaxies and life can form without breaking physical laws. It also links this process to dark energy, the mysterious force causing the Universe to expand faster. The theory builds on earlier ideas about black holes and shows that gravity and heat are connected in surprising ways.

**What’s next step** – The Gravity from Entropy theory is still new and mostly theoretical. Scientists will look for ways to test its predictions by comparing them to observations of the Universe, especially how dark energy changes over time. This could help unite ideas from gravity, quantum physics, and thermodynamics into one bigger picture.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new quantum gravity theory suggests that the Universe’s growth in total disorder allows local order and life to emerge, linking entropy, dark energy, and cosmic complexity.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle théorie explique comment l’Univers devient complexe tout en respectant les lois de la physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps comment l’Univers peut devenir plus complexe, en formant des étoiles, des planètes et la vie, tout en respectant la deuxième loi de la thermodynamique. Cette loi dit que le désordre total, ou entropie, augmente généralement avec le temps, ce qui semble contredire l’apparition d’ordre et de complexité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La mathématicienne Professeure Ginestra Bianconi de l’Université Queen Mary de Londres a étudié ce problème avec une théorie appelée Gravité à partir de l’entropie (GfE). Cette théorie relie la gravité, l’espace et l’information à la plus petite échelle. Ses recherches montrent que, même si l’entropie totale de l’Univers augmente avec son expansion, l’entropie dans chaque petite partie diminue. Cela signifie que des zones locales peuvent devenir plus organisées même si le désordre global grandit.

**Impact** – Cette découverte offre une nouvelle façon de comprendre comment des structures complexes comme les galaxies et la vie peuvent apparaître sans enfreindre les lois physiques. Elle relie aussi ce processus à l’énergie noire, la force mystérieuse qui accélère l’expansion de l’Univers. La théorie s’appuie sur des idées précédentes sur les trous noirs et montre que gravité et chaleur sont liées de manière surprenante.

**Prochaine étape** – La théorie Gravité à partir de l’entropie est encore nouvelle et surtout théorique. Les scientifiques chercheront à tester ses prédictions en les comparant aux observations de l’Univers, notamment sur l’évolution de l’énergie noire. Cela pourrait aider à unir gravité, physique quantique et thermodynamique dans un cadre plus large.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle théorie de la gravité quantique suggère que l’augmentation du désordre total de l’Univers permet l’émergence d’ordre local et de vie, reliant entropie, énergie noire et complexité cosmique.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva teoría explica cómo el Universo se vuelve complejo respetando las leyes de la física
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo cómo el Universo puede volverse más complejo, formando estrellas, planetas y vida, mientras sigue la segunda ley de la termodinámica. Esta ley dice que el desorden total, o entropía, generalmente aumenta con el tiempo, lo que parece contradecir el aumento del orden y la complejidad.

**Qué pasó** – La matemática profesora Ginestra Bianconi de la Universidad Queen Mary de Londres estudió este problema usando una teoría llamada Gravedad desde la Entropía (GfE). Esta teoría conecta la gravedad, el espacio y la información en las escalas más pequeñas. Su investigación mostró que aunque la entropía total del Universo aumenta a medida que el espacio se expande, la entropía dentro de cada pequeña parte disminuye. Esto significa que las zonas locales pueden volverse más organizadas aunque el desorden global crezca.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo ofrece una nueva forma de entender cómo se pueden formar estructuras complejas como galaxias y vida sin romper las leyes físicas. También conecta este proceso con la energía oscura, la fuerza misteriosa que hace que el Universo se expanda más rápido. La teoría se basa en ideas anteriores sobre agujeros negros y muestra que la gravedad y el calor están conectados de formas sorprendentes.

**Próximo paso** – La teoría Gravedad desde la Entropía aún es nueva y principalmente teórica. Los científicos buscarán formas de probar sus predicciones comparándolas con observaciones del Universo, especialmente sobre cómo cambia la energía oscura con el tiempo. Esto podría ayudar a unir la gravedad, la física cuántica y la termodinámica en un marco más amplio.

**Una frase clave** – Una nueva teoría de gravedad cuántica sugiere que el aumento del desorden total del Universo permite que emerja orden local y vida, vinculando entropía, energía oscura y complejidad cósmica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting depicting an abstract cosmic landscape where swirling galaxies and stars emerge gracefully from a flowing, textured fabric of spacetime that subtly resembles interwoven threads of light and shadow, symbolizing the quantum geometric structure; within the expanding space, glowing spheres representing localized pockets of decreasing entropy gently radiate harmony and complexity, while a soft, diffuse glow in the background evokes the dynamic presence of dark energy permeating the universe.

articles.title: Dying Sun-like stars may kick themselves through space
articles.summary: Dying Sun-like stars may not fade away quietly. As blobs of gas erupt unevenly from their swollen surfaces, each burst gives the star a tiny push in the opposite direction. Thousands of these random kicks could eventually break apart distant stellar pairs or, in rare cases, drive two stars into a violent collision.
articles.content:
When stars similar to the Sun grow old, they expand into red giants. During this phase, their outer layers churn and gradually drift into space, while the dense cores left behind shrink into white dwarfs. Because this is the fate of most stars, white dwarfs are among the most common stellar remnants in the universe.

A new model from Caltech theoretical astrophysicist Jim Fuller suggests that this transformation may be far less orderly than it appears. His calculations indicate that uneven bursts of escaping material can repeatedly push a dying star in different directions, giving it thousands of small kicks before the white dwarf fully forms.

Chaotic Eruptions Push Dying Stars

"In this model, blobs of matter are chaotically being ejected from the surface of the bloated stars in an asymmetric fashion," Fuller says. "And every time that happens, the star gets a little kick in the opposite direction. Like Newton said, for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction."

Fuller presented the findings at the 248th meeting of the American Astronomical Society in Pasadena. The study has been submitted to the Publications of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific.

According to Fuller's calculations, a star approaching the white dwarf stage may experience roughly 10,000 small kicks over several hundred thousand years. Each individual push would move the star at only a few meters per second. "That's a slow jogging pace for humans," he says.

Thousands of Kicks Add Up

Although the escaping material is launched in random directions, the kicks do not cancel out perfectly. Over time, they produce an overall shift in one direction through a mathematical process known as a random walk.

A simple comparison is repeatedly flipping a coin to decide whether to move one way or another. Even though each step is random, you will eventually end up some distance from where you began. Fuller's model suggests that the combined kicks could leave a dying star moving in a random direction at about 1 kilometer per second.

Some objects in space receive much stronger pushes. When a massive star explodes as a supernova, for example, the blast can launch the stellar remnant in one direction at high speed. Astronomers have long suspected that white dwarfs also receive kicks, but the process is much gentler because these stars do not explode.

Why Wide Binary Stars Break Apart

Evidence for white dwarf kicks comes from research led by Kareem El-Badry, an assistant professor of astronomy at Caltech. He found that widely separated pairs of stars, known as binaries, are less common after one member of the pair becomes a white dwarf.

The new model offers a possible explanation. A net kick of about 1 kilometer per second could disrupt the orbit of a loosely bound stellar pair, causing the two stars to separate.

"If the orbital speed of the binaries is less than the kick speed, the wide binaries will become gravitationally unbound," Fuller explains.

Fuller developed the model using El-Badry's observations along with computer simulations of convection inside aging red giants. Those simulations show that the churning material near the surface can escape unevenly rather than flowing away in a smooth, balanced pattern.

The model is the first to directly connect many randomly directed ejection events with the motion astronomers suspect white dwarfs receive.

"I am pleased to see a physical model that can explain this observation, which has puzzled me for several years," El-Badry says.

Stellar Kicks Could Trigger Collisions

The model also makes a new prediction. In some binary systems, repeated kicks to a dying red giant could alter its orbit enough to send it crashing into its companion. Such a collision could produce an explosion.

Astronomers may eventually be able to search for signs of these violent stellar mergers, providing a way to test whether Fuller's model accurately describes the final stages of Sun-like stars.

The submitted study titled "White Dwarf Kicks via Episodic Mass Ejection from Red Giant Stars" was funded by Caltech.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a new astrophysical model explaining how dying Sun-like stars receive small random kicks from uneven gas ejections, impacting stellar dynamics such as binary star separations and potential collisions. This is a significant scientific discovery with broad implications for understanding stellar evolution and the behavior of common stellar remnants, white dwarfs. The study is detailed and focused on a single topic, providing meaningful insight into a fundamental cosmic process.
articles.personality_title: New model shows dying stars get tiny pushes that change their paths in space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When stars like our Sun grow old, they swell into red giants and lose their outer layers, leaving behind dense cores called white dwarfs. These white dwarfs are common in the universe, but how they move after forming wasn’t fully understood.

**What happened** – A scientist named Jim Fuller from Caltech created a new model that explains how dying Sun-like stars get tiny pushes from uneven bursts of gas escaping their surfaces. These small pushes, or kicks, happen thousands of times over hundreds of thousands of years and slowly move the star in different directions.

**Impact** – This discovery helps explain why some pairs of stars that orbit each other, called binary stars, sometimes break apart after one becomes a white dwarf. The small kicks can push the stars apart if they orbit each other loosely. In rare cases, the kicks might even cause two stars to crash into each other, possibly causing an explosion.

**What’s next step** – Astronomers will look for signs of these star collisions or changes in star pairs to test if Fuller's model is correct. This could give new clues about how stars end their lives and affect their surroundings.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dying Sun-like stars receive many tiny pushes from uneven gas bursts, which can change their movement and affect nearby stars in surprising ways.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau modèle montre que les étoiles mourantes reçoivent de petites poussées qui changent leur trajectoire dans l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsque des étoiles comme notre Soleil vieillissent, elles gonflent en géantes rouges et perdent leurs couches extérieures, laissant derrière elles des noyaux denses appelés naines blanches. Ces naines blanches sont courantes dans l’univers, mais on ne comprenait pas bien comment elles se déplacent après leur formation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un scientifique nommé Jim Fuller du Caltech a créé un nouveau modèle qui explique comment les étoiles mourantes similaires au Soleil reçoivent de petites poussées dues à des éclats inégaux de gaz qui s’échappent de leur surface. Ces petites poussées, ou coups, se produisent des milliers de fois sur plusieurs centaines de milliers d’années et déplacent lentement l’étoile dans différentes directions.

**Impact** – Cette découverte aide à comprendre pourquoi certaines paires d’étoiles en orbite l’une autour de l’autre, appelées étoiles binaires, se séparent parfois après que l’une devient une naine blanche. Les petites poussées peuvent éloigner les étoiles si leur orbite est lâche. Dans de rares cas, ces coups peuvent même provoquer une collision entre deux étoiles, causant une explosion.

**Prochaine étape** – Les astronomes chercheront des signes de ces collisions d’étoiles ou des changements dans les paires d’étoiles pour vérifier si le modèle de Fuller est correct. Cela pourrait donner de nouveaux indices sur la fin de vie des étoiles et leur influence sur leur environnement.

**En une phrase** – Les étoiles mourantes similaires au Soleil reçoivent de nombreuses petites poussées causées par des éclats inégaux de gaz, ce qui peut changer leur mouvement et influencer les étoiles proches de manière surprenante.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo modelo muestra que las estrellas moribundas reciben pequeños empujones que cambian su camino en el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando estrellas similares al Sol envejecen, se hinchan y se convierten en gigantes rojas, perdiendo sus capas exteriores y dejando núcleos densos llamados enanas blancas. Estas enanas blancas son comunes en el universo, pero no se entendía bien cómo se mueven después de formarse.

**Qué pasó** – Un científico llamado Jim Fuller del Caltech creó un nuevo modelo que explica cómo las estrellas moribundas reciben pequeños empujones debido a ráfagas desiguales de gas que escapan de su superficie. Estos empujones pequeños, o patadas, ocurren miles de veces durante cientos de miles de años y mueven la estrella lentamente en diferentes direcciones.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ayuda a explicar por qué algunas parejas de estrellas que orbitan juntas, llamadas binarias, a veces se separan después de que una se convierte en enana blanca. Los pequeños empujones pueden separar las estrellas si su órbita es débil. En casos raros, estas patadas podrían incluso hacer que dos estrellas choquen, causando una explosión.

**Próximo paso** – Los astrónomos buscarán señales de estas colisiones estelares o cambios en las parejas de estrellas para comprobar si el modelo de Fuller es correcto. Esto podría dar nuevas pistas sobre cómo terminan su vida las estrellas y cómo afectan a su entorno.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las estrellas moribundas similares al Sol reciben muchos pequeños empujones por ráfagas desiguales de gas que pueden cambiar su movimiento y afectar a las estrellas cercanas de formas inesperadas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, glowing red giant star with swirling, uneven bursts of bright material gently ejecting from its surface in multiple directions, surrounded by a faint trail of motion lines suggesting small, repeated kicks propelling the star slowly through a calm, dark space background, with a smaller, dense white dwarf silhouette forming at its core, all rendered in soft, natural reds, oranges, and whites.
